critic_raw:
The methodology is well-constructed overall — temporal splitting, preprocessing leakage prevention, multi-hospital stratification, and label auditing are all handled correctly. However, there are two genuine methodological flaws and one issue that requires empirical resolution.

---

**Issue 1: Post-onset feature leakage via retained treatment-action indicators**

The specific claim: 'The feature set will retain whether sepsis-related treatment actions such as blood culture ordering and antibiotic initiation occurred anywhere within the 12-hour window, even if their timestamps fall after the inferred onset marker; these charted actions are operationally available and may reflect early bedside suspicion before diagnoses are fully documented.'

Why this is wrong: The sepsis label onset time is defined as the earlier of infection evidence (which includes antibiotic initiation with infection diagnosis and positive blood culture) or organ dysfunction. If antibiotic initiation is part of the label definition AND is retained as a feature regardless of where it falls in the 12-hour window — including after the onset marker — then the label and the feature are mutually informative in a direct causal loop. A patient who received antibiotics in hours 10–12 of the window has, by definition, either already crossed the onset threshold (meaning the feature is post-label) or the antibiotic IS the onset trigger (meaning the feature is the label). This is not 'early bedside suspicion captured before documentation' — it is the label event itself, or a direct consequence of it, included as a predictor. The justification ('operationally available') conflates availability in a deployed streaming system with appropriateness in a retrospective training/evaluation study. In a deployed system you would only have the antibiotic event if it had already occurred before your prediction time; in this retrospective setup the 'occurred anywhere in the 12-hour window' formulation allows post-onset events to inform predictions made at admission time.

This leakage asymmetrically inflates both models but inflates the transformer more severely, because the transformer receives the antibiotic/blood culture event as a time-stamped point in the 12-hour sequence and can learn 'antibiotic at hour 10 → sepsis label' trivially, without needing to learn any physiological temporal pattern. The logistic regression baseline also gets a binary indicator, but the transformer can additionally exploit the timing of that event relative to vital sign deterioration, giving it an artificial representational advantage that has nothing to do with temporal pattern learning.

What would constitute evidence: (a) Ablate the treatment-action features entirely and rerun both models; if transformer advantage collapses or reverses, the gain was leakage-driven. (b) Restrict treatment-action features to timestamps strictly before the onset marker and rerun; if performance drops substantially, the window-wide inclusion was doing meaningful predictive work via post-onset events. (c) Compare the feature importance / attention weight on antibiotic/blood culture indicators between sepsis-positive and negative patients; if those features dominate in sepsis-positive cases, that is a red flag.

---

**Issue 2: Comparison confound — architectural inductive bias versus feature representation**

The specific claim: 'This ensures the comparison reflects the benefit of temporal representation learning, not unfair optimization.'

Why this is wrong: The transformer is trained end-to-end on a (12, n_features) tensor at native temporal resolution, while logistic regression operates on hand-engineered scalar summaries. These differ in two ways simultaneously: (a) the representation (raw sequence vs. engineered statistics) and (b) the model family and its inductive biases (attention-based sequence encoder vs. linear model). If the transformer wins, it is impossible to attribute the gain to temporal representation learning specifically, because the transformer also has nonlinear capacity, learned feature interactions, and the ability to weight time steps differentially — none of which are available to logistic regression regardless of how features are engineered. A proper isolation of the hypothesis ('temporal sequence modeling separates future sepsis admissions better than summary-feature modeling') requires an intermediate baseline: a nonlinear model (MLP, gradient boosted trees) trained on the same hand-engineered summaries, or alternatively a logistic regression trained on the full (flattened) raw time series. Without this, the experiment cannot distinguish 'transformers are better because attention' from 'transformers are better because nonlinearity and capacity.'

What would constitute evidence: Add at least one of: (a) gradient boosted trees on the same hand-engineered features, (b) an MLP on the same hand-engineered features, or (c) a simpler RNN/LSTM on the same raw sequence input. If the transformer beats logistic regression but not gradient boosted trees on the same features, the gain is capacity-driven. If the transformer beats all nonlinear baselines on the same features, the gain is representation-driven. Only the latter supports the stated hypothesis.

---

**Issue 3 (empirical): Sepsis prevalence and temporal non-stationarity across the training-to-test horizon**

The specific claim: The temporal split (months 1–24 train, 25–30 val, 31–36 test) mimics prospective deployment.

Why this might fail: Sepsis coding, treatment protocols, and EHR documentation practices evolve over 36–48 months. Changes in antibiotic stewardship, sepsis bundle compliance, or coder behavior (following CMS Sepsis-1 incentives, for example) can shift both the positive rate and the feature distributions in ways that affect the two models asymmetrically. The transformer, which learns temporal micropatterns, may be more sensitive to documentation-frequency changes (e.g., if vitals are measured more often in later months due to a new protocol, the imputation behavior changes and the sequence structure changes). Logistic regression on summary statistics is more robust to this because the statistics smooth over measurement frequency. Conversely, if sepsis prevalence rises in the test window due to a policy change, AUROC is invariant to this but AUPRC and calibration will shift.

This cannot be resolved by analysis alone — it requires inspecting the actual monthly distributions of positive rates, measurement frequencies, and lab missingness across the 36-month span before concluding that the temporal split is a clean prospective proxy.

What would constitute evidence: Plot monthly sepsis prevalence, mean vitals measurement frequency, and lab missingness rate over the full study period. If these are stationary, the temporal split is clean. If they show trends or step-changes, the test set performance reflects distribution shift confound, not prospective generalization.

---

**Summary**: Issue 1 (label leakage via post-onset treatment features) is a genuine flaw that could directly inflate the transformer's apparent advantage and is not correctable by the confound controls described. Issue 2 (missing nonlinear baseline) means the stated hypothesis cannot be confirmed even if the experiment succeeds. Issue 3 requires empirical checking before the temporal split claim can be made.
all_issues_raised: Retaining treatment-action features (antibiotic initiation, blood culture ordering) anywhere in the 12-hour window, including after the sepsis onset marker, constitutes post-onset label leakage because those events are components of the label definition itself.

The transformer-vs-logistic-regression comparison conflates temporal representation learning with model capacity and nonlinearity, because no nonlinear baseline (e.g., gradient boosted trees or MLP) on the same hand-engineered features is included.

Temporal non-stationarity in sepsis coding, treatment protocols, and EHR documentation over the 36-month study period may confound the temporal split's claim to mimic prospective deployment, particularly asymmetrically between the two model families.
all_issues_adjudicated: Retaining treatment-action features (antibiotic initiation, blood culture ordering) anywhere in the 12-hour window, including after the sepsis onset marker, constitutes post-onset label leakage because those events are components of the label definition itself.

The transformer-vs-logistic-regression comparison conflates temporal representation learning with model capacity and nonlinearity, because no nonlinear baseline (e.g., gradient boosted trees or MLP) on the same hand-engineered features is included.

Temporal non-stationarity in sepsis coding, treatment protocols, and EHR documentation over the 36-month study period may confound the temporal split's claim to mimic prospective deployment, particularly asymmetrically between the two model families.
